summary_text:
S:
The patient reports pain in his big toe, describing it as sharp and stabbing. He states he feels a bone spur. He also reports pain in the left hand. The patient may experience heartburn. He reports feeling a little bit of pain when moving the big toe further down and further up. The patient reports pain on the right side of the big toe.

O:
Physical exam reveals tenderness to palpation over the distal phalanx of the right big toe. Range of motion is limited due to pain. Neurological exam is intact. Blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg. Heart rate is 78 bpm.

A:
The patient has foot pain and joint pain. The patient has chest pain.

P:
The patient may have surgery to clean out the joint. A plan will be developed. The patient should see the doctor back in two weeks. The patient will receive an x-ray. The patient may have a shot. The patient should take meloxicam once a day. The patient takes ibuprofen twice a day. The patient wants to test the medicine. The patient wants to test the orthotics.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Right great toe pain.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Joseph Walker is a pleasant 58-year-old male who presents to the clinic today for the evaluation of right great toe pain. The onset of his pain began 2 weeks ago, however it worsened last week. He noticed the pain worsening when he was at a trade show convention and he could not ambulate as he was forced to stand there as the pain was there the whole time. He denies any specific injury. The patient describes his pain usually as throbbing and burning, but notes it occasionally changes to sharp, stabbing pain especially with movement or prolonged ambulation. His symptoms also include redness to the right great toe. The patient states that he has been ambulating on his heel to keep his toe from bending. He reports that his pain is present even with the slightest of pressure, which he notes is worse at night when his sheet is touching his right toe. He adds that he has been taking 2 ibuprofen per day, which does not provide him with relief.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Patient reports that he likes to bicycle ride.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Musculoskeletal: Reports right great toe pain.
Skin: Reports redness.

PHYSICAL EXAM

MSK: Examination of the right great toe reveals 10 degrees of plantar flexion with pain. Pain to palpation of the right great toe, between the big toe and 2nd toe. Palpated a bone spur on the right great toe.

RESULTS

X-ray of the right great toe taken today in office reveals a large bone spur on the anterior aspect of the right great toe joint. There is a loss of cartilage with some arthritis present.

ASSESSMENT

Right foot hallux rigidus.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination and radiographic findings today, I have had a lengthy discussion with the patient in regards to his current symptoms. I have explained to him that his x-rays revealed hallux rigidus. We discussed treatment options for this and I have recommended that we begin with conservative treatment in the form of custom orthotics. I have also prescribed the patient meloxicam once a day to treat the pain. The patient was instructed to discontinue use and contact the office if gastrointestinal issues develop. I advised the patient that I want him to continue his regular activities.

INSTRUCTIONS

The patient will follow up with me in 2 weeks to check on his progress. If his pain does not improve with the orthotics, I will recommend a cortisone injection or surgical intervention.

section_miscategorizations.reason: This is framed as a possible condition rather than a clearly reported symptom; as written, it reads more like a tentative diagnostic impression than a patient-reported complaint.

Chest pain is not supported by the subjective or objective sections and is phrased as a symptom rather than an assessment or diagnosis; if present, it should appear as a patient-reported symptom in S.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient may have surgery to clean out the joint.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is more of a tentative consideration of possible intervention than a concrete management step; such diagnostic/therapeutic contemplation typically belongs in the assessment, with only decided actions in the plan.
This is a meta-comment about clinical reasoning rather than a specific management action; it fits better as part of the assessment narrative than the concrete plan.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient takes ibuprofen twice a day.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This describes an existing medication regimen rather than a new order or change; per instructions, current medications belong in the subjective section.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient wants to test the medicine.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is the patient's preference/subjective stance toward treatment rather than a specific ordered intervention; it is better categorized as subjective information.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient wants to test the orthotics.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This reflects the patient's interest or intent rather than a documented order or prescribed intervention; it is more appropriately subjective content.
